question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who has been granted coarse-grained isAuthorized by maker call setIsRootRatified(maker, arbitraryRoot, true) to ratify a Merkle root containing offers the maker never intended to publish, under PRECONDITIONS where maker has authorized attacker for any reason (e.g., to repay debt), triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, maker) -> attacker calls setterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) -> attacker calls midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, ...) with an offer in maliciousRoot, violating INVARIANT that ratifier approval must bind only offers the maker explicitly authorized, causing scoped impact: unauthorized credit/debt increase in a restricted market? Proof idea: fuzz authorized operator address and root value, assert that isRootRatified[maker][root] can only be true if maker explicitly set it.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an authorized operator call setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) to un-ratify a root that maker legitimately published, under PRECONDITIONS where maker has authorized operator for any unrelated purpose and maker has active offers under that root, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> maker authorizes operator -> operator calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) -> any subsequent take of maker's offer reverts with NotRatified, violating INVARIANT that exit paths must remain available and maker's published offers must remain fillable, causing scoped impact: fund freeze / DoS of maker's offer set? Proof idea: unit test asserting that an authorized operator can flip isRootRatified to false and block all takes on that root.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified / isRatified] Can a formerly-authorized operator leave a permanently ratified root after maker revokes authorization, under PRECONDITIONS where operator was authorized, called setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true), then maker revoked authorization via midnight.setIsAuthorized(operator, false, maker), triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: operator ratifies root -> maker revokes operator -> attacker takes offer using ratified root -> isRatified passes because isRootRatified[maker][root] is still true, violating INVARIANT that ratifier approval must reflect current maker intent, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer fills in restricted market after maker's intent changed? Proof idea: differential test comparing SetterRatifier (persistent ratification) vs EcrecoverRatifier (live authorization check) behavior after revocation.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker exploit the coarse-grained authorization chain (maker -> operatorA -> operatorB) to call setIsRootRatified on behalf of maker, under PRECONDITIONS where maker authorizes operatorA and operatorA uses midnight.setIsAuthorized to authorize operatorB (since authorized accounts can authorize others), triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker authorizes operatorA -> operatorA calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(operatorB, true, maker) -> operatorB calls setterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) -> operatorB takes offer, violating INVARIANT that authorization must only allow intended account delegation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized credit/debt increase via transitive authorization abuse? Proof idea: state test asserting that a two-hop authorization chain allows setIsRootRatified on behalf of maker.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified + midnight.multicall] Can an attacker atomically ratify a root and take an offer in the same multicall transaction, under PRECONDITIONS where attacker is authorized by maker and the market has an enterGate that checks state at call time, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls midnight.multicall([setterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true), midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, ...)]) where the gate check in take() sees the ratification set in the same multicall, violating INVARIANT that gate checks must apply to all entry paths, causing scoped impact: unauthorized credit/debt increase in a gated market bypassing intended access control? Proof idea: fuzz multicall ordering and assert gate invariant holds across all orderings.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker call isRatified directly (not through Midnight) to probe ratification state or trigger side effects, under PRECONDITIONS where attacker calls setterRatifier.isRatified(offer, ratifierData) directly without being MIDNIGHT, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setterRatifier.isRatified(offer, ratifierData) directly, expecting NotMidnight revert, but checking whether any state mutation or information leak occurs before the revert, violating INVARIANT that isRatified must only be callable by MIDNIGHT, causing scoped impact: ratifier bypass if NotMidnight check is bypassable? Proof idea: unit test asserting direct call always reverts with NotMidnight regardless of ratifierData content.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can an attacker supply a crafted leafIndex and proof to pass isLeaf verification for an offer that is NOT in the ratified Merkle tree, under PRECONDITIONS where isRootRatified[maker][root] is true for some root, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker constructs (root, leafIndex, proof) such that HashLib.isLeaf(root, HashLib.hashOffer(offer), leafIndex, proof) returns true for an offer not in the original tree -> attacker calls midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...), violating INVARIANT that ratifier approval must bind the exact offer, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill in restricted market? Proof idea: fuzz (leafIndex, proof, offerHash) triples and assert isLeaf never returns true for offers outside the committed tree.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can an attacker exploit the leafIndex bit-extraction logic (leafIndex >> i) & 1 to produce a second valid proof path for the same offer hash at a different leafIndex, under PRECONDITIONS where a root is ratified and proof.length > 1, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker supplies same offer with different leafIndex and a reordered proof that still hashes to the same root -> isLeaf returns true -> take succeeds, violating INVARIANT that each offer has a unique position in the tree, causing scoped impact: offer replay or double-fill via alternate proof path? Proof idea: differential fuzz test asserting that for a fixed (root, offerHash), at most one (leafIndex, proof) pair passes isLeaf.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can an attacker pass an empty proof (proof.length == 0) with leafIndex = 0 to ratify any offer whose hash equals the root itself, under PRECONDITIONS where isRootRatified[maker][root] is true and root == HashLib.hashOffer(offer), triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker constructs offer such that HashLib.hashOffer(offer) == root -> calls midnight.take(offer, abi.encode(root, 0, new bytes32[](0)), ...) -> isLeaf returns true with empty proof, violating INVARIANT that ratifier approval must bind the exact offer the maker committed to, causing scoped impact: unauthorized take of any offer whose hash the attacker can engineer to match a ratified root? Proof idea: unit test with offer hash == root and empty proof, assert take succeeds only if maker intended single-offer tree.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can an attacker supply leafIndex with high bits set beyond proof.length to bypass the LeafIndexOutOfRange check, under PRECONDITIONS where proof.length is small and leafIndex has bits set above proof.length, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker supplies leafIndex = (1 << proof.length) - 1 + 1 (i.e., leafIndex >> proof.length != 0) -> isLeaf reverts with LeafIndexOutOfRange -> but attacker tries leafIndex values near the boundary to find off-by-one, violating INVARIANT that proof verification must reject all out-of-range indices, causing scoped impact: ratifier bypass via malformed proof index? Proof idea: fuzz leafIndex across [0, type(uint256).max] with fixed proof.length and assert only valid indices pass.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker who is an authorized operator of maker ratify a root for a market with an enterGate, enabling a non-whitelisted taker to increase debt, under PRECONDITIONS where market has enterGate that restricts canIncreaseDebt, maker has authorized attacker, and attacker constructs a sell offer (buy=false) for maker, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> non-whitelisted taker calls midnight.take(sellOffer, ratifierData, units, taker, ...) -> gate check in take() applies to seller (maker) not taker for debt increase -> taker's debt increases if taker is buyer, violating INVARIANT that enter gate must restrict all credit/debt increases, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt increase in gated market? Proof idea: state test with WhitelistGate, assert non-whitelisted buyer cannot increase credit even when ratifier passes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified + take gate check ordering] Can an attacker exploit the ordering of ratifier check before gate check in take() to observe gate state and ratify/un-ratify roots to manipulate gate outcomes, under PRECONDITIONS where enterGate is a stateful contract that changes behavior based on SetterRatifier state, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> attacker calls take() -> gate check reads SetterRatifier state -> attacker uses callback to call setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) mid-take, violating INVARIANT that gate checks must apply consistently throughout take execution, causing scoped impact: gate bypass via mid-take ratification state change? Proof idea: reentrancy test with stateful gate that reads isRootRatified.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for a market with a liquidatorGate to enable unauthorized liquidation, under PRECONDITIONS where market has liquidatorGate restricting canLiquidate, and the liquidation path does not use the ratifier, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> attacker calls midnight.liquidate(market, ...) -> liquidatorGate.canLiquidate(attacker) is checked independently of ratifier, violating INVARIANT that liquidator gate must not be bypassed through alternate liquidation or callback paths, causing scoped impact: unauthorized liquidation in gated market? Proof idea: unit test asserting liquidatorGate check is independent of SetterRatifier state.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker use a maker callback during take to call setIsRootRatified and ratify a new root mid-execution, under PRECONDITIONS where maker has a callback contract that calls setIsRootRatified, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, units, ...) -> maker callback executes -> callback calls setterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, newRoot, true) -> callback calls midnight.take(newOffer, newRatifierData, ...) with newRoot, violating INVARIANT that callbacks must not corrupt partial state through reentrancy, causing scoped impact: unauthorized credit/debt increase via callback-triggered ratification? Proof idea: reentrancy test with maker callback that ratifies and takes a second offer.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker use a flashLoan callback to atomically ratify a root and take an offer without holding any capital, under PRECONDITIONS where attacker has no loan tokens, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls midnight.flashLoan(tokens, assets, attackerCallback, data) -> callback calls setterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> callback calls midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, units, ...) -> callback repays flashloan, violating INVARIANT that gate checks must apply to all entry paths including flashloan-funded paths, causing scoped impact: capital-free unauthorized credit/debt increase in gated market? Proof idea: fuzz flashloan amount and assert gate invariant holds after flashloan-funded take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker who is authorized by maker ratify a root containing a sell offer (buy=false) for a market with enterGate, enabling maker to increase debt post-maturity, under PRECONDITIONS where block.timestamp > market.maturity and sellerDebtIncrease > 0, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> attacker calls midnight.take(sellOffer, ratifierData, units, taker, ...) post-maturity -> take() checks block.timestamp <= offer.market.maturity || sellerDebtIncrease == 0, violating INVARIANT that debt must not increase after maturity, causing scoped impact: unauthorized post-maturity debt increase via ratifier? Proof idea: warp to maturity+1 and assert take with sellerDebtIncrease > 0 always reverts regardless of ratifier state.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for a reduce-only offer (offer.reduceOnly = true) that actually increases credit or debt, under PRECONDITIONS where offer.reduceOnly is true and buyerCreditIncrease > 0 or sellerDebtIncrease > 0, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> attacker calls midnight.take(reduceOnlyOffer, ratifierData, units, ...) -> take() checks !offer.reduceOnly || (offer.buy ? buyerCreditIncrease == 0 : sellerDebtIncrease == 0), violating INVARIANT that reduce-only offers must not increase credit or debt, causing scoped impact: unauthorized credit/debt increase via reduce-only offer bypass? Proof idea: unit test asserting reduce-only check is enforced independently of ratifier state.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for an offer with offer.maker == address(setterRatifier), under PRECONDITIONS where SetterRatifier contract itself is the maker and has been authorized to ratify its own roots, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker deploys SetterRatifier -> attacker calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(setterRatifier), true, address(setterRatifier)) -> attacker calls setterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(address(setterRatifier), root, true) -> attacker calls midnight.take(offer with maker=setterRatifier, ...), violating INVARIANT that maker must be a legitimate participant, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill with SetterRatifier as maker? Proof idea: unit test with SetterRatifier as maker and assert isRatified check passes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker supply malformed ratifierData that causes abi.decode to revert or return unexpected values, under PRECONDITIONS where ratifierData is not a valid ABI encoding of (bytes32, uint256, bytes32[]), triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls midnight.take(offer, malformedRatifierData, units, ...) -> isRatified calls abi.decode(ratifierData, (bytes32, uint256, bytes32[])) -> revert propagates as RatifierFail, violating INVARIANT that malformed input must not cause unexpected state changes before revert, causing scoped impact: DoS of legitimate takes via malformed ratifier data injection? Proof idea: fuzz ratifierData bytes and assert only InvalidProof or NotRatified errors occur, never unexpected state changes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for an offer in market A and then use the same root to ratify an offer in market B (with different enterGate), under PRECONDITIONS where HashLib.hashOffer includes market params so cross-market reuse requires identical market hashes, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker constructs two markets with identical params but different enterGate -> calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) for market A's offer -> attempts to use same ratifierData for market B's offer, violating INVARIANT that ratifier approval must bind the exact market, causing scoped impact: gate bypass via cross-market offer reuse? Proof idea: differential test asserting HashLib.hashMarket includes enterGate and liquidatorGate fields.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for an offer with modified tick (price) compared to what maker intended, under PRECONDITIONS where maker ratified a root for offer at tick T and attacker constructs offer at tick T' with same other fields, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) for root containing offer at tick T -> attacker constructs offer at tick T' -> HashLib.hashOffer(offer') != HashLib.hashOffer(offer) -> isLeaf fails, violating INVARIANT that ratifier approval must bind the exact tick/price, causing scoped impact: unauthorized take at different price? Proof idea: unit test asserting that modifying any offer field changes hashOffer output.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for an offer with maxUnits = type(uint256).max to enable unlimited consumption, under PRECONDITIONS where maker authorized attacker and attacker constructs offer with maxUnits = type(uint256).max, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) for offer with maxUnits = type(uint256).max -> multiple takers call midnight.take(offer, ...) consuming up to type(uint256).max units -> maker's position accumulates unbounded credit/debt, violating INVARIANT that offer fill amount cannot exceed remaining offer capacity, causing scoped impact: unauthorized unbounded credit/debt increase in restricted market? Proof idea: fuzz maxUnits and assert consumed never exceeds maker's intended cap.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for an offer with offer.group = bytes32(0) to share consumption budget with other offers, under PRECONDITIONS where maker has other offers in group bytes32(0) and attacker ratifies a new offer in the same group, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) for offer with group = bytes32(0) -> attacker takes offer -> consumed[maker][bytes32(0)] increases -> maker's other offers in group bytes32(0) are partially consumed, violating INVARIANT that consumption group budget cannot be exceeded across offers, causing scoped impact: unauthorized consumption of maker's group budget? Proof idea: state test asserting that ratifying a new offer in an existing group can exhaust the group budget.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for an offer with offer.expiry = type(uint256).max to create a never-expiring offer, under PRECONDITIONS where maker authorized attacker and attacker constructs offer with expiry = type(uint256).max, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) for offer with expiry = type(uint256).max -> offer remains fillable indefinitely -> attacker or anyone can take it at any future time, violating INVARIANT that offers must respect maker's intended time bounds, causing scoped impact: unauthorized indefinite credit/debt increase in restricted market? Proof idea: warp to far future and assert offer with attacker-set expiry is still fillable.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker ratify a root for an offer with offer.start = 0 to make it immediately active, under PRECONDITIONS where maker authorized attacker and attacker constructs offer with start = 0, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) for offer with start = 0 -> take() checks block.timestamp >= offer.start (always true for start=0) -> offer is immediately takeable, violating INVARIANT that offers must respect maker's intended activation time, causing scoped impact: unauthorized immediate credit/debt increase in restricted market? Proof idea: unit test asserting offer with start=0 is immediately active after ratification.
